questions.q: What is the primary objective of accounting?
opts: (A) Maximizing profits
(B) Providing financial information
(C) Maintaining records
(D) Preparing tax returns
questions.explanation: Accounting provides financial information to stakeholders, enabling them to make informed decisions.

questions.q: What is the difference between cash and accrual basis of accounting?
opts: (A) Cash basis recognizes revenue when received, accrual basis when earned
(B) Accrual basis recognizes revenue when received, cash basis when earned
(C) Both cash and accrual basis recognize revenue when received
(D) Neither cash nor accrual basis recognizes revenue when received
questions.explanation: Cash basis recognizes revenue and expenses when cash is received or paid, while accrual basis recognizes revenue and expenses when earned or incurred.

opts: (A) A report prepared by the management
(B) A report prepared by the auditors
(C) A summary of financial information
(D) A detailed report of transactions
questions.explanation: A financial statement is a summary of financial information, providing a snapshot of a company's financial position and performance.

questions.q: What is the main difference between a ledger and a journal?
opts: (A) A ledger is a permanent record, a journal is temporary
(B) A ledger is temporary, a journal is permanent
(C) A ledger is a summary of transactions, a journal is a detailed record
(D) A ledger is a detailed record, a journal is a summary of transactions
questions.explanation: A ledger is a permanent record of all accounts, while a journal is a temporary record of transactions.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a trial balance?
opts: (A) To prepare financial statements
(B) To verify the accuracy of transactions
(C) To classify accounts into different categories
(D) To test the arithmetical accuracy of ledger accounts
questions.explanation: A trial balance is prepared to test the arithmetical accuracy of ledger accounts, ensuring that the total debits equal the total credits.

opts: (A) Expenses are matched with revenues in the same period
(B) Expenses are matched with revenues in different periods
(C) Revenues are matched with expenses in the same period
(D) Revenues are matched with expenses in different periods
questions.explanation: The matching principle states that expenses should be matched with revenues in the same period, ensuring that the costs of generating revenue are recognized at the same time as the revenue.

questions.q: What is the difference between materiality and disclosure?
opts: (A) Materiality refers to the significance of an item, disclosure refers to its omission
(B) Materiality refers to the omission of an item, disclosure refers to its significance
(C) Materiality refers to the significance of an item, disclosure refers to the inclusion of an item
(D) Materiality refers to the inclusion of an item, disclosure refers to its significance
questions.explanation: Materiality refers to the significance of an item, while disclosure refers to the inclusion of an item in financial statements.

questions.q: What is the purpose of accounting standards?
opts: (A) To ensure consistency in accounting practices
(B) To ensure comparability of financial statements
(C) To ensure that financial statements are prepared in a specific format
(D) To ensure that financial statements are prepared in a specific way
questions.explanation: Accounting standards ensure that financial statements are prepared in a comparable manner, enabling stakeholders to make informed decisions.

opts: (A) A business that is not expected to continue operating in the future
(B) A business that is expected to continue operating in the future
(C) A business that is in the process of liquidation
(D) A business that is in the process of merger
questions.explanation: A going concern is a business that is expected to continue operating in the future, enabling the use of accrual accounting and long-term financial planning.

questions.q: What is the main difference between current assets and non-current assets?
opts: (A) Current assets are expected to be converted into cash within a year, non-current assets are not
(B) Current assets are not expected to be converted into cash within a year, non-current assets are
(C) Current assets are expected to be converted into cash within a year, non-current assets are expected to be converted into cash within a few years
(D) Current assets are not expected to be converted into cash within a year, non-current assets are expected to be converted into cash within a few years
questions.explanation: Current assets are expected to be converted into cash within a year, while non-current assets are not expected to be converted into cash within a year.

questions.q: What is the main difference between current liabilities and non-current liabilities?
opts: (A) Current liabilities are expected to be paid within a year, non-current liabilities are not
(B) Current liabilities are not expected to be paid within a year, non-current liabilities are
(C) Current liabilities are expected to be paid within a year, non-current liabilities are expected to be paid within a few years
(D) Current liabilities are not expected to be paid within a year, non-current liabilities are expected to be paid within a few years
questions.explanation: Current liabilities are expected to be paid within a year, while non-current liabilities are not expected to be paid within a year.

questions.q: What is the difference between accruals and prepayments?
opts: (A) Accruals refer to amounts received in advance, prepayments refer to amounts paid in advance
(B) Accruals refer to amounts paid in advance, prepayments refer to amounts received in advance
(C) Accruals refer to amounts owed but not yet received, prepayments refer to amounts paid but not yet used
(D) Accruals refer to amounts owed but not yet received, prepayments refer to amounts received but not yet used
questions.explanation: Accruals refer to amounts owed but not yet received, while prepayments refer to amounts received but not yet used.

questions.q: What is the purpose of depreciation?
opts: (A) To increase the value of an asset
(B) To decrease the value of an asset
(C) To maintain the value of an asset
(D) To ignore the value of an asset
questions.explanation: Depreciation is the systematic reduction in the value of an asset over its useful life, reflecting its decreasing ability to generate revenue.

questions.q: What is the difference between straight-line method and written-down value method of depreciation?
opts: (A) Straight-line method assumes a constant rate of depreciation, written-down value method assumes a variable rate of depreciation
(B) Straight-line method assumes a variable rate of depreciation, written-down value method assumes a constant rate of depreciation
(C) Straight-line method assumes a constant rate of depreciation, written-down value method assumes a decreasing rate of depreciation
(D) Straight-line method assumes a decreasing rate of depreciation, written-down value method assumes a constant rate of depreciation
questions.explanation: Straight-line method assumes a constant rate of depreciation, while written-down value method assumes a variable rate of depreciation.

questions.q: What is the purpose of impairment loss?
opts: (A) To increase the value of an asset
(B) To decrease the value of an asset
(C) To maintain the value of an asset
(D) To ignore the value of an asset
questions.explanation: Impairment loss is the reduction in the value of an asset due to deterioration in its quality or decrease in its market value.

questions.q: What is the difference between FIFO and LIFO methods of inventory valuation?
opts: (A) FIFO assumes that the first items purchased are sold first, LIFO assumes that the last items purchased are sold first
(B) FIFO assumes that the last items purchased are sold first, LIFO assumes that the first items purchased are sold first
(C) FIFO assumes that the items purchased are sold in the order of their purchase, LIFO assumes that the items sold are the ones purchased last
(D) FIFO assumes that the items sold are the ones purchased last, LIFO assumes that the items purchased are sold in the order of their purchase
questions.explanation: FIFO assumes that the first items purchased are sold first, while LIFO assumes that the last items purchased are sold first.

questions.q: What is the main difference between cash flow statement and balance sheet?
opts: (A) Cash flow statement shows the inflows and outflows of cash, balance sheet shows the financial position of a company
(B) Cash flow statement shows the financial position of a company, balance sheet shows the inflows and outflows of cash
(C) Cash flow statement shows the inflows and outflows of cash, balance sheet shows the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents
(D) Cash flow statement shows the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents, balance sheet shows the financial position of a company
questions.explanation: Cash flow statement shows the inflows and outflows of cash, while balance sheet shows the financial position of a company.

questions.q: What is the main difference between direct and indirect method of presenting cash flows?
opts: (A) Direct method shows the inflows and outflows of cash, indirect method shows the net increase in cash and cash equivalents
(B) Direct method shows the net increase in cash and cash equivalents, indirect method shows the inflows and outflows of cash
(C) Direct method shows the inflows and outflows of cash, indirect method shows the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents
(D) Direct method shows the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents, indirect method shows the inflows and outflows of cash
questions.explanation: Direct method shows the inflows and outflows of cash, while indirect method shows the net increase in cash and cash equivalents.

questions.q: What is the main difference between current ratio and acid-test ratio?
opts: (A) Current ratio is calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities, acid-test ratio is calculated by dividing cash and cash equivalents by current liabilities
(B) Current ratio is calculated by dividing cash and cash equivalents by current liabilities, acid-test ratio is calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities
(C) Current ratio is calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities and prepaid expenses, acid-test ratio is calculated by dividing cash and cash equivalents by current liabilities
(D) Current ratio is calculated by dividing cash and cash equivalents by current liabilities and prepaid expenses, acid-test ratio is calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities
questions.explanation: Current ratio is calculated by dividing current assets by current liabilities, while acid-test ratio is calculated by dividing cash and cash equivalents by current liabilities.
